source.title: NPNF-211. Sulpitius Severus, Vincent of Lerins, John Cassian

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: “In the same way, some time afterwards, we made a journey with him while he visited the various parishes in his diocese.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: He had gone forward a little by himself, some necessity or other, I know not what, compelling us to keep behind.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: In the meantime, a state-conveyance, full of military men, was coming along the public highway.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: But when the animals near the side beheld Martin in his shaggy garment, with a long black cloak over it, being alarmed, they swerved a little in the opposite direction.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: Then, the reins getting entangled, they threw into confusion those extended lines in which, as you have often seen, those wretched creatures are held together; and as they were with difficulty rearranged, delay, of course, was caused to those people hastening forward.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: Enraged by this injury, the soldiers, with hasty leaps, made for the ground.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: And then they began to belabor Martin with whips and staves; and as he, in silence and with incredible patience, submitted his back to them smiting him, this roused the greater fury in these wretches, for they became all the more violent from the fact, that he, as if he did not feel the blows showered upon him, seemed to despise them.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: He fell almost lifeless to the earth; and we, ere long, found him covered with blood, and wounded in every part of his body.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: Lifting him up without delay, and placing him upon his own ass, while we execrated the place of that cruel bloodshed, we hastened, off as speedily as possible.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: In the meantime, the soldiers having returned to their conveyance, after their fury was satisfied, urge the beasts to proceed in the direction in which they had been going.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: But they all remained fixed to the spot, as stiff as if they had been brazen statues, and although their masters shouted at them, and the sound of their whips echoed on every side, still the animals never moved.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: These men next all fall to with lashes; in fact, while punishing the mules, they waste all the Gallic whips they had.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: The whole of the neighboring wood is laid hold of, and the beasts are beaten with enormous cudgels; but these cruel hands still effected nothing: the animals continued to stand in one and the same place like fixed effigies.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: The wretched men knew not what to do, and they could no longer conceal from themselves that, in some way or other, there was a higher power at work in the bosoms of these brutes, so that they were, in fact, restrained by the interposition of a deity.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: At length, therefore, returning to themselves, they began to enquire who he was whom but a little before they had scourged at the same place; and when, on pursuing the investigation, they ascertained from those on the way that it was Martin who had been so cruelly beaten by them, then, indeed, the cause of their misfortune appeared manifest to all; and they could no longer doubt that they were kept back on account of the injury done to that man.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: Accordingly, they all rush after us at full speed, and, conscious of what they had done and deserved, overwhelmed with shame, weeping, and having their heads and faces smeared with the dust with which they themselves had besprinkled their bodies, they cast themselves at Martin’s feet, imploring his pardon, and begging that he would allow them to proceed.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: They added that they had been sufficiently punished by their conscience alone, and that they deeply felt that the earth might swallow them alive in that very spot, or that rather, they, losing all sense, might justly be stiffened into immovable rocks, just as they had seen their beasts of burden fixed to the places in which they stood; but they begged and entreated him to extend to them pardon for their crime, and to allow them to go on their way.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: The blessed man had been aware, before they came up to us, that they were in a state of detention, and had already informed us of the fact; however, he kindly granted them forgiveness; and, restoring their animals, permitted them to pursue their journey.
